title: Beef vindaloo
subtitle: Fiery braised beef in Goan vinegar-chilli sauce
excerpt: Beef braised in the vindaloo marinade (wine vinegar, large quantities of chilli, garlic, and warming spices) until the meat is tender and deeply permeated with the hot, sour sauce. The vinegar and chilli combination is the Goan signature; the long braise converts the beef collagen into a thick, clinging sauce. The hottest standard option on the curry-house menu, applied to beef.
sourceNotes: Beef vindaloo applies the Goan vindalho technique to beef braising cuts. In Goa the dish traditionally uses pork; the British-Indian adaptation is more commonly made with lamb or beef. Documented in British Indian restaurant literature from the 1970s as the standard maximum-heat curry.

content.text: Beef absorbs the vindaloo marinade more deeply than lamb because of its denser muscle fibre structure. The vinegar in the marinade begins to denature the surface proteins before cooking begins, which helps the spice flavours penetrate. Marinate for at least 2 hours; overnight is better. The finished sauce should smell of vinegar and chilli in equal measure, neither should dominate.

The braise for beef vindaloo runs 80 to 90 minutes, longer than for lamb. The beef should be completely tender, almost falling apart, so that the dark, fiery sauce has permeated all the way to the centre of each piece. A vindaloo where the outside is flavoured and the interior is plain has not been cooked long enough.
Combine the {{braising-steak}} with the {{white-wine-vinegar}}, 1 teaspoon of the {{chilli-powder}}, and {{turmeric}}. Toss well and refrigerate for at least 2 hours, or overnight.

Heat the {{vegetable-oil}} in a large heavy pan over high heat. Remove the beef from the marinade (reserving the marinade) and brown in batches for 3 minutes per batch. Remove and set aside. Reduce heat to medium. Add the {{onion}} and cook for 12 minutes until golden. Add the {{garlic}}, {{ginger-root}}, remaining {{chilli-powder}}, {{cumin-ground}}, and {{coriander-ground}} and stir for 1 minute. Add the {{tinned-tomatoes}} and reserved marinade and cook for 10 minutes until thickened. Return the beef and add 250 ml water. Bring to the boil, cover, and simmer for 80 to 90 minutes until the beef is completely tender. Remove the lid for the final 15 minutes to reduce. Stir in the {{garam-masala}} and season with salt.

items.fix: Chuck and braising steak are the correct cuts. If the liquid has evaporated, add water in 100 ml increments and continue at a proper simmer, the collagen conversion requires sustained heat
items.cause: Temperature too low or too lean a cut

items.fix: The three teaspoons of chilli powder assumes medium-heat chilli powder. If using mild, add half a teaspoon more. Taste at the end and adjust
The tomato base should reduce to a thick paste before the beef is returned, this concentrates the sauce and balances the acidity. A thin sauce amplifies the vinegar note
items.cause: Marinade not balanced by the tomato base

items.fix: Remove lid for the full final 15 minutes at medium heat to reduce the sauce to a coating consistency
items.cause: Lid on too long without sufficient reduction

content.text: Beef vindaloo is made less often than pork vindaloo (the Goan original) or lamb vindaloo (the British-Indian standard). The braising beef version has a strong claim to being the best of the three. The collagen-rich cuts turn rich and silky over the long braise. The vinegar-chilli marinade soaks deeper into beef than into pork or lamb. The dish rewards patience and a day-ahead start more than almost any other curry on the list.
